data.description:
## Why

The MCP `run_workflow` docstring in `src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py` claims the run
"executes in the background so it survives client timeouts" and "Run-state
persists durably ... (resumable on crash)". The implementation is a
`threading.Thread(target=_bg, daemon=True)` (around `_mcp_writes.py:297`). A
daemon thread does NOT survive process exit, and there is no reaper or
`workflow resume` entrypoint that re-drives an orphaned run — so if the MCP host
exits, the run is stranded at `running` forever. The docstring oversells a
durability guarantee the code does not provide. Since MCP tools are
model-controlled, that inaccuracy can mislead an agent into relying on
non-existent crash-durability.

## What

Correct the honesty of the durability wording (documentation only — the durable
runner itself is explicitly OUT of scope):

- Rewrite the `run_workflow` docstring to state true semantics: the background
  work runs on a **daemon thread that does not survive MCP-process exit**; there
  is **no automatic reaper**; step records are persisted with idempotency keys so
  a run **can be resumed only if re-invoked**, but nothing auto-resumes it.
- Align any adjacent claims that imply automatic crash-resumption
  (e.g. `llm/workflow/runs.py` "durable; resumable" phrasing) so they describe
  the persistence substrate, not an automatic guarantee.

## Files

- `src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py` (the `run_workflow` docstring)
- `src/rebar/llm/workflow/runs.py` (align wording if it overstates)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] The `run_workflow` docstring no longer implies the daemon thread survives
  process exit or that runs auto-resume after a crash.
- [ ] It accurately states: daemon thread, no reaper, resume requires
  re-invocation, step records are idempotent.
- [ ] Any adjacent "resumable on crash" wording is aligned to describe the
  persistence substrate, not an automatic guarantee.
- [ ] No runtime/behavior change; `make lint` stays green.
data.title: Fix run_workflow durability docstring to state true crash semantics
